questions.stem: For each finding, click to specify whether the finding is more consistent with pheochromocytoma crisis or less consistent with pheochromocytoma crisis.
questions.scenario: The client reports recurrent episodes of severe headache and pounding palpitations.

options.text: More consistent with pheochromocytoma crisis
Less consistent with pheochromocytoma crisis

questions.rationale: Pheochromocytoma crisis is solved faster when the learner bundles headache, diaphoresis, palpitations, and severe hypertension as a catecholamine surge pattern. Chronic, non-episodic findings push the answer away from this diagnosis.

distractorRationales.a: More consistent with pheochromocytoma crisis misses the clinical pattern: Pheochromocytoma Crisis hypertension points to the row-specific answer pattern, so choosing it can postpone safety actions and worsen clinical safety risk.
distractorRationales.b: Less consistent with pheochromocytoma crisis misses the clinical pattern: Pheochromocytoma Crisis hypertension points to the row-specific answer pattern, so choosing it can postpone safety actions and worsen clinical safety risk.

questions.takeaway: Headache, diaphoresis, palpitations, and severe episodic hypertension belong together as a pheochromocytoma pattern.
questions.speedCue: The speed clue is episodic sympathetic surge, not slow chronic symptom drift.

questions.stem: Which conclusion should guide the nurse's next move for this client with kidney injury?
questions.scenario: A client with acute kidney injury reports weakness and nausea.
questions.additionalInfo: Telemetry shows peaked T waves and the potassium level is 6.8 mEq/L.

options.text: Hyperkalemia emergency requiring immediate cardiac stabilization and potassium-lowering treatment
Expected fatigue from hospitalization that only needs rest
Mild electrolyte change that can wait for the next lab draw
Simple dehydration without urgent membrane risk

questions.rationale: Peaked T waves plus marked hyperkalemia change this into a cardiac-stability emergency. The nurse should answer from conduction risk first, not from symptom management alone.

distractorRationales.d: Simple dehydration without urgent membrane risk misses the clinical pattern: Hyperkalemia Ecg Emergency potassium points to Hyperkalemia emergency requiring immediate cardiac stabilization and potassium-lowering treatment, so choosing it can postpone safety actions and worsen circulation or perfusion.

questions.takeaway: Telemetry changes are what make hyperkalemia urgent at the bedside.

questions.stem: Which conclusion should direct the nurse's next move after this chemotherapy change?

Simple nausea from treatment without true metabolic collapse

questions.stem: Which decision map best explains this client's rapid deterioration?
questions.scenario: A client with chronic adrenal insufficiency has been vomiting for 24 hours and now looks weak and confused.

options.text: Adrenal crisis requiring immediate steroid replacement, fluid support, and shock-focused escalation
Simple dehydration that only needs oral fluids
Expected fatigue from chronic illness without emergency risk
A benign stomach virus that can wait for clinic follow-up

distractorRationales.d: A benign stomach virus that can wait for clinic follow-up misses the clinical pattern: Adrenal Crisis Hypotension shock points to Adrenal crisis requiring immediate steroid replacement, fluid support, and shock-focused escalation, so choosing it can postpone safety actions and worsen circulation or perfusion.

questions.stem: Which clinical map should guide the nurse's next move after this transfusion starts?

questions.stem: Which decision map best explains this change after fracture care?
questions.scenario: A client with a new lower-leg cast reports escalating pain that is not relieved by opioids.

options.text: Compartment syndrome requiring urgent pressure-relief escalation and neurovascular protection

Routine swelling without perfusion concern
Simple anxiety because the cast feels tight

questions.stem: Which decision map should guide the nurse's next move after this epidural change?

distractorRationales.d: Normal labor discomfort that only needs reassurance misses the clinical pattern: Epidural Hypotension Fetal perfusion points to Maternal hypotension after epidural requiring perfusion rescue to protect fetal oxygenation, so choosing it can postpone safety actions and worsen oxygenation or ventilation.

questions.stem: Which decision map should guide the nurse's first move for this child?
questions.scenario: A child arrives leaning forward, drooling, with a muffled voice and inspiratory stridor.

options.text: Epiglottitis pattern requiring airway protection and no throat stimulation
Simple sore throat that only needs an oral exam and reassurance

Behavioral distress without true airway danger

distractorRationales.d: Behavioral distress without true airway danger misses the clinical pattern: Epiglottitis Airway Protection airway points to Epiglottitis pattern requiring airway protection and no throat stimulation, so choosing it can postpone safety actions and worsen oxygenation or ventilation.

questions.takeaway: When the child is drooling and quiet with stridor, think airway first, not assessment curiosity.

questions.stem: Which decision map best explains this late-pregnancy change?
questions.scenario: A pregnant client with worsening hypertension reports severe right-upper-quadrant pain and nausea.

options.text: HELLP syndrome requiring urgent maternal-fetal escalation and hemorrhage-risk awareness
Expected indigestion late in pregnancy
A benign gallbladder problem without obstetric urgency
Normal musculoskeletal pain from uterine growth

questions.rationale: RUQ pain with falling platelets, liver injury, and worsening hypertension creates a HELLP map. The nurse should answer from maternal-fetal emergency risk, not from reflux or benign discomfort.

distractorRationales.b: Expected indigestion late in pregnancy misses the clinical pattern: Hellp Syndrome hypertension points to HELLP syndrome requiring urgent maternal-fetal escalation and hemorrhage-risk awareness, so choosing it can postpone safety actions and worsen bleeding or anticoagulation injury.

questions.takeaway: RUQ pain in preeclampsia stems is a clue you should not soften into heartburn.

questions.stem: Which decision map should guide the nurse's next move for this postpartum client?

options.text: Magnesium toxicity requiring infusion stop, airway support, and antidote-focused escalation

questions.stem: Which clinical map best explains this client's immediate post-anesthesia change?
questions.scenario: Shortly after surgery, a client develops muscle rigidity, rapidly rising end-tidal CO2, tachycardia, and a temperature that is climbing.
questions.additionalInfo: The team is debating whether the fever is the main clue or a later clue.

options.text: Malignant hyperthermia requiring trigger removal, dantrolene, cooling, and aggressive support
Routine postoperative shivering that only needs blankets
Expected anxiety from emergence from anesthesia
Simple dehydration without a true anesthetic emergency

questions.rationale: The fast clue is rigidity with rising end-tidal CO2 after anesthesia. The nurse should answer from malignant hyperthermia rescue rather than waiting for temperature to become the only obvious signal.

distractorRationales.b: Routine postoperative shivering that only needs blankets misses the clinical pattern: Malignant Hyperthermia Initial tachycardia points to Malignant hyperthermia requiring trigger removal, dantrolene, cooling, and aggressive support, so choosing it can postpone safety actions and worsen infection progression.

distractorRationales.d: Simple dehydration without a true anesthetic emergency misses the clinical pattern: Malignant Hyperthermia Initial tachycardia points to Malignant hyperthermia requiring trigger removal, dantrolene, cooling, and aggressive support, so choosing it can postpone safety actions and worsen infection progression.
